title: Hutongs And Lakes
subtitle: Prince Kung / Shichahai / towers
blurb: Start with princely courtyards, then move to water, towers, and quieter turns.
mood: Courtyards, lakes, narrow streets, and weather choices

microcopy.line: If the lake or lanes are packed, use a parallel street or a short ride.

content.intro:
This day shows the northern old center without the weight of a palace day: Prince Kung Mansion, the Shichahai edge, Yandai Xiejie, the Drum Tower, and Wudaoying. Slow down where there are doors, old trees, and water, but do not make every lane mandatory.

Crowds by the lakes, heat, and rain can break the rhythm. If the stretch from Shichahai to Wudaoying becomes tiring, do not push through; metro or DiDi will protect the evening better than two more ordinary kilometers.
content.routeLogic:
At Prince Kung Mansion, walk courtyards and garden before entering the lanes.
At Shichahai, choose the shore by shade and crowd density.
Use Yandai Xiejie as a short passage toward the towers.
At the Drum Tower, decide whether the climb is worth it.
Finish in Wudaoying where metro or taxi is easy.
content.bestFor:
the day after palaces
old streets, water, and short stops
photos along the way without a heavy museum plan
content.notFor:
travelers who need even sidewalks all day
heavy rain
when narrow-street crowds irritate you quickly
content.beforeYouGo:
Check Prince Kung Mansion hours and closed day.
Weekends are tighter around the lakes and Yandai Xiejie.
In summer, move long walking stretches to morning or evening; in rain, keep a cafe or metro option nearby.
content.timing: Start before lunch at Prince Kung Mansion. Reach the lakes when you want open air, leave the tower for orientation, and keep Wudaoying as a calmer finish before dinner.
content.fallbackPlan: If the lanes wear you down, take metro or taxi from Shichahai toward Wudaoying. In heavy rain, keep Prince Kung Mansion, a short lake edge, and a covered meal nearby.

content.context: Inside Prince Kung Mansion, walk the courtyards first, then the garden and rocks. After that, the neighboring hutongs feel less random and more like a continuation of the old city. If groups block a narrow passage, use the garden edges.
content.lookFor:
Sequence of courtyards
Garden rocks
Changes between enclosed and open space
Where groups block the way
content.practical:
The museum closes on Mondays except holidays.
Tickets are sold ahead through official channels.
Do not stand behind a dense group in a narrow passage.
content.nextMove: Walk toward Shichahai, choosing quieter lanes between the larger streets.
content.sourceNote: Prince Kung Mansion has hours, a closed day, and advance ticketing; check the official channel before visiting.

stopList.name: Shichahai
stopList.note: Stay by the shore only while it is not tight

content.context: At Shichahai, choose your side by shade, wind, and crowd density. If the waterfront is loud or rickshaws crowd the lane, step into a nearby alley and return to the lake after a few turns.
content.lookFor:
Bridges and reflections
Courtyard entrances near water
The shift between bars and residential lanes
Where the shore gets too tight
content.practical:
In heat, stay on the shaded side.
Skip a full loop if the shore is packed.
The edge can be slippery after rain.
content.nextMove: Continue toward Yandai Xiejie, but do not turn the street into a long shopping stop.

stopList.name: Yandai Xiejie
stopList.note: Pass through briefly without shopping pressure

content.context: Yandai Xiejie is short and often dense. Notice the bend of the street, older facades between signs, and side hutong passages, but move on if the flow stops.
content.lookFor:
The curve of the street
Older facades among shop signs
Side passages
Where you can leave the crowd
content.practical:
Keep your bag close in dense flow.
Choose food by queue movement and freshness.
If the street is overloaded, use parallel lanes.
content.nextMove: Head toward the Drum and Bell Towers as the next large landmark.

stopList.name: Drum Tower
stopList.note: Decide on the spot whether to climb

content.context: At the towers, stop on the square and check the map: water stays south, hutongs surround you, and Wudaoying is farther east. Climb only if visibility is decent and you still have energy.
content.lookFor:
Silhouettes of Drum and Bell Towers
The square between streets
Low roofs around you
The entrance queue
content.practical:
Hours and interior access change by season.
The climb can be steep.
If you are tired, keep to the square and move toward food or metro.
content.nextMove: From the towers, walk or ride toward Wudaoying depending on weather and energy.
content.sourceNote: The towers have seasonal hours and ticket-sale cutoffs before closing.

stopList.name: Wudaoying Hutong
stopList.note: A cafe finish with quick transport out

content.context: In Wudaoying, slow down and look beyond cafes: side passages, old doors, and low roofs still appear between the signs. If the main lane feels too commercial, turn onto a neighboring street and return to metro from there.
content.lookFor:
Old doors
Low roofs
Side passages
Where cafes give way to ordinary street life
content.practical:
Evenings can be tight.
Do not enter private courtyards without a clear invitation.
If you are tired, finish at the metro instead of adding another district.
content.nextMove: End at the metro or take DiDi to dinner in another district.
